schools.summary: Matthew Henry sketches the sons of the prophets as humble, poor, industrious men, content with mean things; poverty is no bar to prophecy.
Ellicott: 'where we sit before thee' is the hall of instruction; the disciples were no monkish community but could marry, and their number had simply grown.
Clarke: despite Israel's profligacy the schools of the prophets increased, owing to Elisha's influence.
Gill, with the Targum, reads 'sons of the prophets' as the disciples of the prophets.
Ellicott and Keil: the well-wooded Jordan valley; 'where we may dwell' is 'to sit there' for instruction; the axe was 'begged,' not merely borrowed.
Gill locates Jordan some six miles from Gilgal, on Josephus's reckoning.
Wesley: though devoted to religious exercises, the sons of the prophets sometimes employed themselves in manual arts.
Gill: they begged Elisha's company for order among themselves, advice on the site, and protection from men and wild beasts.
Ellicott: 'be content' is 'consent'; Elisha is asked to go to superintend the work and help in any difficulty.
Matthew Henry: good disciples desire to be under good discipline, acting in weighty matters by permission of their superiors.
Gill: the riverside trees were felled for the building's rafters and flooring, the walls presumably of stone.
Ellicott: the Hebrew is 'the timber' which they required.
Matthew Henry: Elisha's going with them shows no minister should think himself too great to stoop and do good.
Ellicott and Keil: the iron is fronted in the accusative to mark the mishap; the cry 'Master!' is an appeal for help, and the tool was 'begged' rather than borrowed.
Clarke, with Jarchi: 'Alas, my master, and it was sought' — the loss cuts deeper because he has nothing to repay it.
Matthew Henry: be as careful of the borrowed as of one's own; poverty's sorest grief is being unable to pay a just debt.

sides.position: 'begged' — asked of a neighbour, which deepens the shame
'borrowed,' so that repayment is now owed

schools.summary: Keil rejects the rationalist 'stick lifted the axe' reading as itself impossible; Ellicott makes the stick a symbolic act, and both hear God's care for small personal troubles.
Clarke: the wood had no power to raise iron; it was a sign, the real miracle being wrought against the metal's gravity.
Gill: Elisha's question shows the prophet did not know all things at all times, or asked to lead on to the miracle.
Matthew Henry: as God made iron swim, so his grace can raise the stony iron heart sunk in the mud of the world.

debates.question: What is the role of the stick Elisha cast in?

sides.position: a mere sign or symbolic ceremony with no natural power
only the occasion of divine power; the natural explanation is itself impossible

schools.summary: Gill: 'Take it up' is spoken to the very man who lost the axe, so the griever is the recoverer.
Keil and Ellicott treat the verse as the hinge closing the axe-head episode and opening the Syrian war.
Ellicott reads continuous border-warfare and wrestles with the corrupt phrase 'my camp' (perhaps 'lie in ambush' or 'go down'); Keil: flying raids that Elisha repeatedly exposed.
Matthew Henry: God knows even the deepest-laid devices; the king heeds warnings of the Syrians but not of his sins.
Clarke: probably the same Ben-hadad; a restless prince always finds a pretext, and he laid ambushes by watching Israel's movements.
Gill notes the ground of the war is not stated.

debates.question: How should the corrupt word rendered 'my camp' be read?

sides.position: 'set an ambush' / 'lie in wait,' following the versions
'ye shall go down,' i.e. on a plundering raid

schools.summary: Clarke: Elisha had the intelligence by immediate revelation from heaven, not by any human scout.
Ellicott defends the received 'are come down' against the versions' 'are lurking'; Keil: the king acted on the warnings and saved himself repeatedly.
Gill: it was Elisha who sent the warning to the king of Israel.

debates.question: Does the anomalous word mean 'are come down' or 'are lurking/hidden'?

sides.position: 'are come down,' the received text, from the verb 'to descend'
'are lurking / in ambush,' with the Septuagint, Syriac, Vulgate, and Targum

schools.summary: Gill: the king sent spies to verify the prophet's word, having no hearty respect for Elisha.
Clarke (with the Vulgate) and Wesley: the king sent troops to pre-occupy and secure the threatened place, forestalling the ambush.
Ellicott: he sent a sufficient force to hold the place; 'saved himself' means he was wary, and this happened more than once.

debates.question: What did the king do on receiving the warning?

sides.position: sent spies to verify the prophet's word, showing grudging faith
sent troops to occupy and secure the place, forestalling the Syrians

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'troubled' means storm-tossed; the unique 'which of us' may be read 'who betrays me' or 'who leads us astray'; Keil reads a demand to expose a partisan of Israel.
Gill: the king's heart was like a troubled sea, exceeding uneasy at his repeated disappointments.

debates.question: What does the king's unique demand mean?

sides.position: 'Which of us is for the king of Israel?' — a traitor among his own men
'Who leads us astray / betrays me to the king of Israel?' on a re-pointing

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: Elisha continually reveals the Syrian's bedchamber counsels; the king then sends to Dothan, twelve Roman miles north of Samaria.
Matthew Henry: the Syrians unwittingly confess the boundless knowledge of Israel's God, from whom nothing done, said, or thought is hidden.
Gill: the king had thought all his counsellors faithful, so the servant corrects his suspicion.

themes: the God who hears in the bedchamber

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: it is folly to send a host against one who could discover and defeat any plot; those who fight against God's prophet know not what they do.
Clarke, Ellicott, and Keil locate Dothan twelve miles from Samaria on a hilltop pass, and describe the great host sent by night to surround it.
Gill: how could the king hope to seize one who could read his very designs?

themes: the folly of seizing the prophet

schools.summary: Ellicott: the 'great host' was a company of infantry, not an army, come by night to surprise the city.
Clarke: strange the king never thought that one who read his secrets could also learn of the plot against his life.
Gill: the show of horses and chariots was meant to terrify the town into surrendering the prophet.
Matthew Henry: why so great a force against one unguarded man? It measures the king's misjudgement.

themes: the folly of force against the prophet

schools.summary: Gill and Ellicott: the servant is a new attendant, not Gehazi, who rose early and saw the encircling host; Keil looks ahead to the opening of his eyes upon the unseen world.
Matthew Henry: had the servant studied the Psalms he would not fear a host, nor doubt a master by whom God had done great things.
Matthew Henry: spoken to all God's servants amid fightings and fears; correct fear by high thoughts of God and the invisible world.
Clarke and Wesley: the whole heavenly host was commissioned to help Elisha; the allies are angels unspeakably more numerous, and God infinitely more powerful.
Ellicott sets the assurance beside Numbers 14:9, Psalms 3:6, and Hezekiah's word in 2 Chronicles 32:7.
Gill: those with them are the legions of angels encamped around.

themes: more with us than with them

schools.summary: Ellicott: as at Elijah's translation and Balaam's angel, the servant's eyes are opened; the fiery form answers the Syrians' own chariots, fire being the token of Jehovah's presence. Keil: a translation into sight of the spirit-world.
Matthew Henry: the strong must compassion the weak; the fiery guard were God's angelic hosts, and opened eyes silence our fears.
Clarke: the fiery horses were already there before the eyes were opened; Wesley: fire that could both terrify and consume.
Gill: the eyes opened were those of the mind — the servant was granted a vision.

debates.question: Are the servant's opened eyes bodily or of the mind?

sides.position: the eyes of the mind — a granted vision
real sight of what was objectively there all along

schools.summary: Ellicott: the Syriac and Josephus read 'they went down to them'; the affliction is a 'dazing' bewilderment (as in Genesis 19:11), not true blindness — 'they saw, but knew not what they saw.'
Clarke: their sight was confounded so they could not know what they saw or mistook one place for another.
Gill: the Syrians came down the hill and Elisha went out to meet them and to proceed toward Samaria.

debates.question: Did the Syrians come down to Elisha, or he to them?

sides.position: Elisha and his servant went down to them (Syriac, Josephus)
the Syrians came down to him

schools.summary: Ellicott canvasses the readings and reconciles them: the dazed Syrians were wandering, so 'this is not the way' was literally true; the promise to show 'the man' is irony.
Matthew Henry: Elisha did not lie, for he had left the city; those who fight God are justly given up to delusions.
Gill: the words were true though ambiguous, since Elisha had come out and was going to Samaria.

debates.question: Were Elisha's words a falsehood or literally true?

sides.position: a lawful ruse against an enemy, though a falsehood
literally true, given that Elisha had left the city and the Syrians were wandering

schools.summary: Ellicott: the supernatural bewilderment explains how the host was led in unguarded; when their eyes opened, dismay paralysed them.
Matthew Henry: as Satan blinds men to their ruin, so opened eyes show these foes captive in the midst of their enemies; God's enemies are triumphed over when they think to triumph.
Clarke: the prayer removed their confusion so they could discern things as they were.
Gill: their eyes were opened that they might know where they were.
Ellicott: 'my father' honours the prophet; the doubled question shows eagerness to slay, yet the king asks Elisha's permission.
Clarke: to smite them would be dastardly; the most he could honourably do was make them prisoners of war.
Matthew Henry: the king reveres the prophet and may recall Ahab's fatal clemency, refusing to strike without commission.

debates.question: How should the king's eagerness to smite be judged?

sides.position: a cowardly bloodthirst against defenceless men
eagerness restrained by reverence and memory of Ahab's error

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: these are prisoners of war, not to be slain in cold blood; killing them would frustrate the miracle's purpose of making Syria own the true God.
Clarke and Wesley: sparing and feeding foes is at once singular piety and charity, honouring true religion, and prudence to mollify Syrian hearts.
Matthew Henry: where Elijah showed divine justice by fire, Elisha shows divine mercy, heaping coals to melt his enemies; overcome evil with good.
Gill: the captives were not the king's but the prophet's, or rather the Lord's.
Matthew Henry: the most glorious victory is to turn an enemy into a friend; the Syrians saw it was vain to assault so great and good a man.
Clarke: the freed men would report God's power and the king's magnanimity, ending war for a time; Wesley: they came no more 'for some considerable time.'
Ellicott: only the raiding 'bands' ceased; the Syrians felt no gratitude but resolved on open war with their whole army.
Gill: the king's great feast showed his obedience to the prophet's orders.

debates.question: Why did the Syrian raids cease?

sides.position: an enemy melted by mercy into peace
a mere tactical shift from raids to open war, without gratitude

themes: turning a foe into a friend

schools.summary: Gill: unsoftened by kindness, Ben-hadad gave up ambush and mustered all his forces for open war.
Matthew Henry: base spirits never feel obliged; the ungrateful Syrians came to starve, not storm, the city.
Wesley: Ben-hadad, whom Ahab wickedly spared, returns to fulfil the divine prediction against that clemency.
Ellicott identifies Ben-hadad II, who besieged Samaria under Ahab, and traces his name on Assyrian monuments; Keil marks the start of the siege and famine.
Keil and Ellicott reckon the famine prices (ass's head about ten pounds) and cite Plutarch and Pliny; the ass was unclean and its head the least edible part.
Clarke and Wesley take 'dove's dung' as a coarse pulse or pease (the chick-pea), Bochart concluding for a kind of pulse; the siege was too sudden for storing food.
Gill: perhaps no care had been taken to lay up stores against the siege.

sides.position: a kind of pulse or pease (chick-pea) so named for its shape and colour
literal dove's dung, eaten in extremity

themes: the worthlessness of money in famine

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil: the king walked the broad rampart to conduct the defence and encourage the garrison when the woman appealed to him from below.
Matthew Henry: whither should a subject in distress go but to the prince, the protector of right and avenger of wrong?
Gill: the king was on the wall to observe the enemy and see his soldiers do their duty.

themes: a cry from the famished city

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil: the right sense is 'if the Lord do not help thee, whence shall I?' — Jehoram, in the irony of despair, points to the exhausted floor and winepress.
Wesley: creatures are helpless without God; every creature is only what God makes it.
Matthew Henry: a lament of the king's disability to help unless God help, though some hear a fretful, cross-grained word.
Gill notes the marginal 'let not the Lord save thee' and Josephus's report that the king cursed her in God's name.

debates.question: Is the king's reply a curse or a plain 'if the Lord do not help thee'?

sides.position: an imprecation upon the woman ('let not the Lord save thee')
a genuine 'if the Lord do not help thee, whence shall I?'

schools.summary: Ellicott sets the horror beside the covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:56) and the later cannibalism in the sieges of Jerusalem.
Matthew Henry: a barbarous agreement in which appetite overpowers a mother's natural love — a fulfilment of the threatened judgment (Deuteronomy 28:53).
Gill: the king's passion subsides into pity as he asks what ails her.
Gill: the threatened judgment begins to be accomplished, as later at the sieges of Jerusalem.
Wesley: the curse of apostasy now fallen; Clarke: the children likely died of hunger first, and (with Jarchi) the second mother hid her son to eat him alone.
Ellicott: 'she hath hid her son' — perhaps to save him, love reasserting itself.

debates.question: Why did the second mother hide her son?

sides.position: from greed, to eat him unshared (with Jarchi)
to save him, a mother's love returning

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: the hidden sackcloth, worn next the skin, was a secret self-humbling to appease Jehovah, and a portent to his subjects; the covenant curses were now fulfilled.
Matthew Henry: he lamented the calamity but not his sin, its procuring cause; instead of reforming, he swears Elisha's death.
Clarke: the king was in deep mourning for the distresses of his people.
Gill: he rent his clothes at the horror of the report and grief for his people.

debates.question: Is the king's sackcloth true repentance or mere despairing woe?

sides.position: a secret self-humbling to appease Jehovah
grief over consequences, not sin — no true contrition

schools.summary: Ellicott: a common oath-form; Elisha had likely urged resistance in faith that Jehovah would help, yet brought no relief — the oath echoes Jezebel's against Elijah.
Matthew Henry: instead of pulling down the calves, he swears the death of the most innocent head in Israel — the reflex of blaming the messenger.
Clarke: the desperate king either blamed Elisha for the calamity or thought he could remove it and would not.
Gill: the king swore dreadful imprecations against the prophet.
Ellicott and Keil: the elders gathered to seek Jehovah's will; the inward-opening door was to be held against the executioner; 'son of a murderer' marks Joram's descent from Ahab, slayer of Naboth.
Matthew Henry: Elisha sits composed while the king rages; the harsh epithet is warranted only by his extraordinary prophetic commission.
Wesley: the harsh words are not to be imitated, but Elisha rebukes as an extraordinary prophet in the name of the King of kings; Clarke thinks the king came to watch the beheading.

debates.question: Why must the door be held against the messenger?

sides.position: the king was following to revoke the order and repent
the king came to see the execution carried out

schools.summary: Ellicott adopts Ewald's reading 'the king' for 'messenger': the king comes down in despair, minded to surrender and slay the prophet.
Matthew Henry: the king's premise (all penal evil is of the Lord) is true, but his inference — to wait for God no longer — is the outrage of despair, unlike patient Eli, David, and Job.
Clarke: uncertain who speaks; perhaps Elisha, hinting deliverance is at hand; men lay their suffering on any but themselves, and the chapter is wrongly divided from the relief that follows.

debates.question: Who speaks 'this evil is of the Lord; why wait for him longer?'

sides.position: the king, come down in despair (reading 'king' for 'messenger')
possibly Elisha answering the messenger at the door

themes: 'why wait for the Lord any longer?'
all evil is of the Lord
